title: High Point vs Wisconsin — First Round Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • First Round • Portland, OR
extraInfo: Winner faces #4 Arkansas or #13 Hawaii on Saturday

stakes.callout: This is the 12-vs-5 upset everyone's circling. A team from the Furniture Capital of the World that scores 90 a game and leads the nation in steals, against a Big Ten program that's been reinvented as a three-point shooting factory. High Point has the longest winning streak in the country (14 games). Wisconsin hasn't reached the Sweet 16 since 2017. Something's gotta give.

cards.label: For High Point
cards.value: This is only their second NCAA Tournament appearance ever. They lost to Purdue in the first round last year. A win would be the biggest moment in program history — and they've got the style to pull it off.

cards.label: For Wisconsin
cards.value: The Badgers have lost in the first or second round in three of their last four tournaments. They haven't made the Sweet 16 since 2017. Greg Gard has never reached the Elite Eight. The pressure is real.

cards.label: Style Clash
cards.value: High Point leads the nation in steals (11.1/game). Wisconsin has the lowest steal rate against in the entire country. This is literally the unstoppable force vs. the immovable object.

cards.label: The Upset Math
cards.value: Since 1985, 12-seeds have beaten 5-seeds 36% of the time. It's the most common "upset" in the tournament. ESPN analysts have already flagged this as their preferred 12-over-5 pick.

styles.title: Havoc-Ball Scoring Machine
bullets: Offense: High Point averages 90.0 PPG (4th nationally) at the 49th-fastest tempo in D-I. They emphasize two-point scoring (57.1% on twos, 33rd nationally) and get to the foul line constantly (26.3 FTA/game). They are 15-0 when scoring 90+.

Steal everything: They lead the nation in steals per game (11.1), rank 2nd in fast-break points, and 3rd in turnovers forced. Their turnover margin of +7.5 is the best in D-I. They turn defense into instant offense.
Ball security: Here's what makes them scary — they force turnovers without committing many. They rank 15th nationally in turnover percentage. It's not reckless chaos. It's controlled chaos.

The portal worked: Eight of the top nine scorers are transfers. Rob Martin (SEMO), Cam'Ron Fletcher (Kentucky/FSU/Xavier), Owen Aquino (Liberty), Conrad Martinez (Arizona). This is a collection of talented guys who couldn't find the right home — until now.

Weakness: Defensive efficiency is 161st nationally — when teams don't turn it over, they score. Schedule strength ranks 342nd. Zero Quad-1 wins. No starter above 6'8". This is a massive step up in competition.

styles.title: Three-Point Factory
bullets: Volume shooting: Wisconsin makes 11.3 threes per game (1st in Big Ten) and takes 31.9 attempts (9th nationally). Over half their field goal attempts come from three. They are undefeated when scoring 80+.

The backcourt: Nick Boyd (20.3 PPG) and John Blackwell (19.0 PPG) are one of only five high-major backcourts where both guards average 18+. Wisconsin is 9-0 when both score 20+. Boyd is the first Badger to average 20 since Michael Finley in 1995.

Ball security: Wisconsin has the 2nd-lowest offensive turnover rate in the country and the lowest steal rate against in the nation. This is their best weapon against High Point's press — they simply don't give the ball away.

Nolan Winter is back: The 7-footer missed the Big Ten Tournament with an ankle sprain but is confirmed available. At 13.3 PPG and 8.6 RPG, he gives Wisconsin an interior presence that High Point (no starter above 6'8") can't match.

Weakness: Defense allows 75.9 PPG (236th nationally). Transition defense is a particular vulnerability — and that's exactly where High Point thrives. If the Panthers get steals and run, Wisconsin's defense could buckle.

keys.title: The turnover battle is the whole game.
keys.description: High Point leads the nation in steals. Wisconsin has the lowest steal rate against in the country. This is the defining statistical collision. If High Point can disrupt Wisconsin's ball handlers and create transition opportunities, they can absolutely win. If Wisconsin protects the ball like they have all season, High Point's biggest weapon gets neutralized and this becomes a half-court game — where the Badgers have a huge advantage.

keys.title: Can High Point handle the size disadvantage?
keys.description: Wisconsin has a 7-footer (Nolan Winter, 13.3 PPG, 8.6 RPG). High Point's tallest starter is 6'8" (Owen Aquino). That's a massive mismatch in the paint. If Winter is healthy and active, he could dominate the glass and force High Point into uncomfortable defensive rotations. High Point needs Aquino's versatility — and maybe some hack-a-Winter at the free throw line — to survive this.

keys.title: Pace, pace, pace.
keys.description: High Point wants chaos — 90 PPG, 78.8 possessions per game. Wisconsin wants controlled aggression — 83 PPG in a half-court-oriented offense. If the game is played in the 80s with lots of possessions, High Point has a chance. If Wisconsin can slow it to the 70s and make every possession matter, their superior shooting efficiency takes over.

keys.title: Can Boyd and Blackwell handle the pressure?
keys.description: Wisconsin's backcourt duo averages a combined 39.3 PPG. But they've never seen anything like High Point's full-court defensive pressure — 11.1 steals per game. Boyd and Blackwell are experienced and poised, but the Big Ten doesn't play this style. How they handle the first five minutes of press will set the tone.

keys.title: The experience factor cuts both ways.
keys.description: Wisconsin has 8 Quad-1 wins, three victories over top-10 teams, and a Big Ten Tournament run. High Point has a 342nd-ranked schedule, zero Quad-1 wins, and lost both Quad-2 games by 17. But High Point has NCAA Tournament experience from last year's loss to Purdue, a roster ranked 36th nationally in experience, and a 14-game winning streak. Confidence matters in March.

prediction.summary: This is the 12-over-5 upset everyone's circling, and the statistical collision at its heart is irresistible: High Point leads the nation in steals (11.1/game), Wisconsin has the lowest steal rate against in the entire country. Something has to give. The smart money says Wisconsin's ball security wins this battle — Boyd and Blackwell are too experienced and poised to get rattled by full-court pressure they've never seen in the Big Ten. But High Point's havoc defense creates a different kind of chaos, and if the Panthers can force even 3-4 extra turnovers in transition, they'll generate the easy baskets that keep this close. The size mismatch with Nolan Winter (7-0) is real — High Point has no one above 6'8" — but Aquino's 45.5% three-point shooting from the 4-spot can pull Winter out to the perimeter and neutralize the interior advantage. Wisconsin's 236th-ranked defense (75.9 PPG allowed) is genuinely vulnerable to a team that scores 90.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: High Point comes out pressing and the pace is frenetic. Martin picks up a steal and scores in transition. Fletcher grabs an offensive rebound and dunks it. The Panthers score 20 in the first 8 minutes and lead 20-16. Wisconsin looks uncomfortable with the tempo, and Boyd has his first turnover against the press.

phases.title: Mid-First Half to Halftime
phases.description: Wisconsin settles in. Boyd and Blackwell start exploiting High Point's 161st-ranked half-court defense, hitting pull-up threes and finding Winter inside. Carrington comes off the bench and hits two quick threes. The Badgers close the half on a 14-6 run and lead 41-38 at halftime. Both teams are scoring at will.

phases.title: Second Half — The Shootout
phases.description: High Point refuses to go away. Anderson bullies his way to 6 quick points inside, and Martin orchestrates the offense with poise. Aquino drills a three that pulls Winter out to the perimeter and opens the lane. High Point ties it at 58 with 10 minutes left. The upset is very much alive. Wisconsin calls timeout.

phases.title: Final 8 Minutes
phases.description: Boyd takes over. The first Badger to average 20 since Michael Finley scores 10 points in the final 8 minutes, including a step-back three and two free throws. High Point answers with Fletcher's athleticism and Martin's floaters, but Winter's 7-foot frame produces two crucial blocks down the stretch that High Point's undersized lineup can't overcome. Wisconsin survives, but the Panthers push them to the absolute limit.

projectedScore.rationale: Wisconsin's ball security neutralizes High Point's steals, and Boyd's late-game scoring proves decisive. But High Point's tempo and balanced attack keep this terrifyingly close — exactly the kind of 5-12 game that ages you ten years.

players.name: Rob Martin
players.meta: G • Big South Newcomer of the Year • Riley Wallace Award Finalist
players.bio:
Transferred from Southeast Missouri State, where he was First Team All-OVC. Named a finalist for the Riley Wallace Award (most impactful transfer in the nation). Had 24 points in the Big South championship game and averaged 19.3 PPG across the conference tournament. Maintains a 2.4 assist-to-turnover ratio and once had 8 steals in a single game. He's the engine.
Read more →

players.name: Terry Anderson
players.meta: F • 6-6 • All-Big South • Big South Tournament MVP
players.bio: The Big South Tournament MVP transferred from Lamar and has become High Point's most physical player. He shoots 56.4% from the field and does his damage inside, bullying smaller defenders. Had 22 points and 12 rebounds against SC Upstate. At 6'6", he plays bigger than his size.

players.name: Cam'Ron Fletcher
players.bio: The ultimate basketball wanderer: Kentucky → Florida State → Xavier → High Point. A former four-star recruit and Missouri Class 4 Player of the Year, Fletcher finally found a home and is having the best season of his career. Had 17 points and 19 rebounds in the Big South championship game. Sometimes the fifth stop is the charm.

players.name: Owen Aquino
players.meta: F • 6-8 • Madrid, Spain • Team Block Leader
players.bio: A unicorn from Madrid: a 6'8" forward who leads the team in blocks AND shoots 45.5% from three. Came through Eastern Florida State (NJCAA All-American, FCSAA Player of the Year) and Liberty before High Point. His rim protection and three-point shooting from the 4-spot is rare at any level — and he'll need both against Wisconsin's 7-footer.

players.name: Nick Boyd
players.bio:
The first Badger to average 20+ PPG since Michael Finley in 1995. Scored double figures in all 31 games with 21 games of 20+. Career-high 36 against Providence (7 reb, 7 ast, 0 turnovers — the only such stat line in the NCAA in 15 years). Set the Wisconsin Big Ten Tournament scoring record with 38 points against Illinois. Averages 22.7 PPG against AP Top 25 teams.
Read more →

players.name: John Blackwell
players.meta: G • 6-4 • Third-Team All-Big Ten • NBA Draft Prospect
players.bio: The junior from Bloomfield Hills, Michigan, whose father Glynn played four seasons at Illinois. Tested the 2025 NBA Draft waters before returning. Ranks 11th on Wisconsin's all-time scoring list. He and Boyd scored 31 and 38 in the Big Ten Tournament OT win over Illinois — the first Badger duo to both score 30+ since 1968.

players.name: Nolan Winter
players.bio:
The 7-footer from Lakeville, Minnesota, whose father Trevor was a 7-foot center on Minnesota's 1997 Final Four team and whose mother played volleyball for the Gophers. Has 12 double-doubles this season. Suffered an ankle sprain March 4 and missed the Big Ten Tournament — but posted "Go Time" on Instagram and is confirmed available. His return is massive against a High Point team with no one above 6'8".
Read more →

players.name: Braeden Carrington
players.meta: G • 6-5 • Minnesota Mr. Basketball • Program Record 9 Threes
players.bio: Named Minnesota's Mr. Basketball in high school, then played at Minnesota and Tulsa before Wisconsin. Set the program single-game record with 9 made threes against Washington, scoring 32 off the bench. When he gets hot, he changes the game in minutes. Aspires to become a sports agent after basketball.

funFacts.title: The Furniture Capital of the World (High Point)
funFacts.body: High Point, North Carolina, hosts the High Point Market — one of the world's largest furniture trade shows, drawing 75,000+ visitors twice a year. The city is literally the furniture capital of the world. Now its basketball team is trying to furnish a trophy case.

funFacts.title: Why They're Called Badgers (Wisconsin)
funFacts.body: Wisconsin's nickname comes from the state's early lead miners who burrowed into hillsides and "lived like badgers" to stay warm. The school used to bring live badgers to games before switching to a costumed mascot in 1949. Bucky's full name? Buckingham U. Badger.

funFacts.title: $2 Billion Campus Transformation (High Point)
funFacts.body: HPU president Nido Qubein, a Lebanese-American businessman, has invested over $2 billion in the campus since 2005. Enrollment is up 322%. Campus acreage up 515%. The arena is named after him and his wife. He basically built a new university.

funFacts.title: From Slowest to Fastest (Wisconsin)
funFacts.body: Under Bo Ryan, Wisconsin played at the slowest tempo in America (345th in 2015). Under Greg Gard, they now take the 9th-most three-point attempts nationally. Former star Frank Kaminsky said: "It's weird to watch for me, we played so slow." More →

funFacts.title: From Kentucky to the Furniture Capital (High Point)
funFacts.body: Cam'Ron Fletcher was a 4-star recruit who played at Kentucky, Florida State, and Xavier before finding his best basketball at a small private school in North Carolina. He had 17 points and 19 rebounds in the Big South championship game. Fifth time's the charm.

funFacts.title: Medal of Honor at 17 (High Point)
funFacts.body: HPU alumnus Jack Lucas received the Medal of Honor at age 17 — the youngest Marine to receive the honor in WWII. He threw himself on two grenades at the Battle of Iwo Jima to save his squadmates. He survived.

funFacts.title: The Spanish Connection (High Point)
funFacts.body: High Point has TWO players from Spain: Owen Aquino from Madrid (who shoots 45.5% from three as a 6'8" big man) and Conrad Martinez from the Joventut Badalona youth system (who played at Arizona). The Furniture Capital has gone international.

funFacts.title: Father-Son Final Four (Wisconsin)
funFacts.body: Center Nolan Winter's father Trevor was a 7-foot center on Minnesota's 1997 Final Four team. His mother Heidi played volleyball for the Gophers. Now Nolan's trying to take Wisconsin on a tournament run of his own.

funFacts.title: From the NBL to the NCAA (Wisconsin)
funFacts.body: Austin Rapp played in Australia's NBL at age 18, winning a national championship. Now he's at Wisconsin hitting five straight threes in the Big Ten Tournament. He described his younger self: "Five years ago, I was a little chubby kid running around, not doing much." More →

funFacts.title: Tubby Smith's Alma Mater (High Point)
funFacts.body: NCAA champion coach Tubby Smith (1998 Kentucky national title) is a High Point alumnus. He even returned to coach at HPU from 2018-21, going 36-41. The program has come a long way since.

funFacts.title: NASCAR Connection (High Point)
funFacts.body: HPU alumnus Austin Dillon drives the No. 3 Chevrolet in the NASCAR Cup Series for Richard Childress Racing. Country star Donna Fargo ("The Happiest Girl in the Whole U.S.A.") also went to High Point.

funFacts.title: Garding Against Cancer (Wisconsin)
funFacts.body: Coach Greg Gard and his wife Michelle founded "Garding Against Cancer" in 2016, raising over $9 million for UW's Carbone Cancer Center. His son Isaac is a walk-on guard on the current roster.

history.callout: Wisconsin has 29 NCAA Tournament appearances, a national championship (1941), and back-to-back Final Fours (2014-15). High Point made the tournament for the first time in program history last year. This is David vs. Goliath — except David scores 90 a game and leads the nation in steals.

timeline.text: Wisconsin wins its only national championship under coach Bud Foster.
As an 8-seed, the Badgers upset No. 1 Arizona, No. 6 LSU, and No. 1 Purdue to reach the Final Four. Lost to eventual champion Michigan State.
Wisconsin reaches the Final Four but loses to Kentucky 74-73 on Aaron Harrison's last-second three — one of the most heartbreaking shots in tournament history.
The Badgers return to the Final Four and end Kentucky's undefeated season (38-0) with a 71-64 victory. Frank Kaminsky: 20 pts, 11 reb. Lost to Duke 68-63 in the national championship game.
Wisconsin hasn't reached the Sweet 16 since 2017. Recent early exits: lost as a 5-seed to James Madison (2024), lost to BYU in the Round of 32 (2025). The drought is real.

subsections.title: High Point's Rapid Rise
subsections.content:
Before 2025, High Point had never appeared in the NCAA Tournament. The program bounced around lower divisions and the Big South without ever cutting down the nets. Then Alan Huss won three straight Big South regular-season titles and the first-ever conference tournament championship in 2025, earning a 13-seed and a date with Purdue.

The Panthers lost 75-63, but the taste of March was transformative. When Huss left for Creighton, Flynn Clayman inherited just one returning starter, rebuilt through the portal, and delivered a program-record 30 wins, a 14-game winning streak, and a return trip — this time as a 12-seed with legitimate upset potential.

The investment of president Nido Qubein — $2+ billion into campus since 2005 — has been the foundation. The Qubein Arena opened in 2021. The basketball program is now keeping pace with the school's transformation.

subsections.title: The 5-vs-12 Upset Tradition
subsections.content:
The 5-vs-12 matchup is the most common upset in tournament history. Since the field expanded to 64 teams in 1985, 12-seeds have won roughly 36% of the time. Recent examples: James Madison over Wisconsin (2024), Furman over Virginia (2023), Richmond over Iowa (2022). Wisconsin fans know this fear intimately.

High Point's profile — fast tempo, havoc defense, experienced roster, long winning streak — matches almost every successful 12-seed upset formula. The question is whether their 342nd-ranked schedule has left them unprepared for this moment.
